critique.summary: The article is a personal account of the author's week-long intensive preparation for a Google technical interview using a Large Language Model (LLM) as a tutor. The author describes their struggles with algorithmic concepts, their learning process guided by the LLM, and the eventual interview experience, including successes and challenges faced under stress.
mainPoints: The author received an unexpected interview opportunity from Google and had only one week to prepare.
They used an LLM (Gemini Pro chat) as a private tutor to learn algorithmic concepts and solve LeetCode problems without focusing on code initially.
The author progressed from simple to medium and some hard algorithmic problems, gaining insights into data structures, graph traversal, and dynamic programming.
Despite preparation, the author struggled with stress and memory lapses during the interview, particularly with iterative binary search implementation.
The interview resulted in an invitation to on-site technical interviews, with feedback to improve code debuggability.
The author reflects on the benefits and limitations of using an LLM for learning algorithms and the importance of human teaching and fluency in coding.
critique.truthfulness: The article is a subjective personal narrative and does not make factual claims requiring verification beyond the author's experience. The described use of an LLM for learning algorithms and the interview process aligns with known practices and challenges faced by candidates preparing for technical interviews. The author's reflections on algorithmic concepts, interview formats, and the role of AI tutoring are plausible and consistent with common knowledge about software engineering interviews and AI-assisted learning.
considerations: The article does not provide external sources or perspectives, limiting its journalistic reliability.
It does not discuss alternative preparation methods or compare the effectiveness of LLM tutoring against traditional study or human mentorship.
The impact of stress on interview performance and strategies to mitigate it are mentioned but not deeply analyzed.
The article does not clarify the outcome of the on-site interviews or long-term effects of the preparation approach.
There is no technical validation of the LLM's correctness or the quality of solutions provided.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the use of large language models (LLMs) for learning, interview preparation, and their impact on traditional education and hiring practices. Several comments critique the effectiveness, ethics, and cultural implications of relying on AI tools.

comments.author: gurachek
comments.text: Your "no compiler" rule on day 3 taught you more than the LLM did. The LLM made concepts click. But the binary search vanishing under interview stress proves that understanding something and being able to produce it under pressure are totally different skills. Nobody talks about this enough in the "just use ChatGPT to learn" discourse.
comments.analysis: Highlights the distinction between theoretical understanding and practical performance under pressure, emphasizing limitations of AI in replicating real-world skills.

comments.author: doctorpangloss
comments.text:
another POV is, it used to be cool to work for Google

it's been so uncool and harrowing for a while now, to deal with their leetcode BS. i mean obviously this guy is well meaning but didn't learn anything, other than for the paycheck and whatever desperate circumstances require that.

the LLM stuff being used to solve their interview process is an inflection point where it really steeply goes down to want to work for Google for any reason other than money

maybe this is why Google deepmind researchers keep leaving to start their own successful companies
comments.analysis: Expresses a cynical view on Google's hiring culture, suggesting that reliance on leetcode and AI tools diminishes genuine skill development and job satisfaction.

comments.author: piokoch
comments.text:
This is very interesting, I've been using LLM to learn new things that way and it really worked. To some extent, learning with LLM is better than taking any course, even with a tutor, as I am getting something prepared for me, in terms of my experience, progress level, etc.

LLM is going to change schools and universities a lot, teachers, tutors will have to find themselves in the new reality, as they have a strong competitor with infinite resources and huge knowledge, patient and ready to work with every student in a distinct way, according to student's needs, level, intelligence, etc.

Instruction-based tutoring is dead from that perspective, why should I follow someone reciting a book or online tutorial, while there is a tool that can introduce me into subject in a better and more interesting way?

Sure, there are great teachers, who are inspiring people, who are able to present the topic in a great way, the point is, they are minority. Now, everyone can have a great tutor for a few dollars a month (or for free, if you don't need generating too much data quickly).
comments.analysis: Provides a positive personal account of using LLMs for self-directed learning, supporting the idea that AI can supplement or replace traditional education methods.

comments.author: tom-blk
comments.text: Very cool, I have personally been studying zk-cryptography with a similar approach, works really well with some caveats. Will save this article and try this version as well when the time comes!
comments.analysis: Shares a practical application of the discussed approach, indicating its usefulness in specialized technical learning, though with some reservations.

comments.author: e12e
comments.text:
Interesting article - but perhaps a bit light on details in some places, like:

> I generated a list of the most common interview tasks

How? I suppose they mean gathered, or searched for, not strictly generated?

Also a little light on details of the actual interview.

I'm also a little confused about the listing of "problems" - do they refer to some specific leet-code site's listing of problems?

It seems like half-way between naming an actual algorithm/problem and naming a concrete exercise.

As for:

> How is it that we do not use this "forgotten and forbidden" coding in our daily production code, even though all highly reusable, useful code is essentially an exploitation of the intersection between classical algorithmic thinking and real-world problems?

I'm not sure what to say - most of this stuff lives in library code and data structure implementations for any language in common use?

Indeed the one saving grace of leet code interview is arguably that it shows if the candidate can choose sane data structures (and algorithms) when implementing real-world code?
comments.analysis: Critiques the article for lacking detailed methodology, which limits the ability to assess the validity of the claims made about interview task generation.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: The honest ones who admit they used it as a learning tool rather than a shortcut are getting more useful out of it than anyone else.
comments.analysis: Highlights the value of transparency in using AI tools for genuine learning, implying that honesty enhances the educational benefit.
